data.title: 2026 Playbook: My Market Outlook Is To Bet On The Consumer And Double Down On AI
data.description:
Introduction

Looking out into 2026, many institutions have already made bold claims into a possible price target for the S&P 500 for the end of 2026. While some institutions chose more aggressive targets, like the price target of $8,000 by Deutsche Bank, and others have more conservative numbers, such as BofA, with a price target of $7,100, the message is still clear. With a current SPX price of just under $6,900, it is evident that in some way, analysts expect upside from our current position.

I largely agree with the above projections, however, some of my reasoning may go against the grain of the overall market.

In this article, I would like to introduce the three key pillars of my investment thesis, that I believe will drive returns. While I do believe other catalysts exist, I want to focus this article explicitly on some topics that may not have been covered to the same level of depth as I would like to see. These three pillars are:

  • Numerous tax cuts will create a Covid-like Stimulus Check environment, boosting the economy via consumer spending
  • Rhetoric of consumer weakness is significantly overstated
  • Unprecedented capital expenditures create a tangible "Follow the Money" action plan, completely avoiding the speculative risk of predicting specific AI market leaders

From these three core statements, I would like to rate the market a "Buy" for 2026, with a price target of $7,700 on the S&P 500, in line with many consensus estimates of major institutions.

OBBBA Tax Cuts Driving Consumer Strength

With the signing of the OBBBA (One Big Beautiful Bill Act) on July 4, 2026, President Trump was able to enact a number of new provisions for many Americans.

One key aspect of these tax provisions is that they backdate until January 1, 2025. This means that until the signing of the OBBBA, many Americans have been overpaying their taxes, and as such, need to be returned their excess taxes.

Some of the most critical tax benefits that have been introduced center around:

  • No tax on tips
  • No tax on overtime
  • No tax on auto loan interest
  • Increases to standard and SALT (State and Local Taxes) deductions

According to JP Morgan, this is expected to create a surge in tax returns in early 2026, driven largely by the backdated tax returns mentioned above.

While this factor is obviously not new, I believe that it has largely gone unappreciated by the market, as I have not seen much mention of this in discussion with peers or others in online blogs, such as Seeking Alpha.

According to the same article, this additional refund is expected to drive a meaningful increase to GDP due to increased consumer spending, with estimates ranging from 0.5-0.8% in the first quarter of 2026 alone.

This means that in some ways, this situation is similar to the Covid Stimulus Checks, albeit on a much smaller scale. However, unlike Covid, the economy is obviously not being wrangled out of a generational-level recession, with second quarter 2020 GDP levels of -28% on an annual basis. Instead, the economy is trailing at a relatively healthy 3.8% annual rate, with Q3 2025 GDP expected to come in around 3.2% on December 23.

Therefore, relative to the situation of the current economy, I strongly believe that this Stimulus Check in the form of a tax return will create a meaningful boost to consumer spending, and as such, the overall economy.

While it has been argued that OBBBA disproportionally targets wealthier consumers, regardless of this debate, it is evident that even the consumers that are struggling the most in the current environment will be able to benefit at least to some degree.

Additionally, the expected tax return can be expected to be even larger for the 2026 tax return. While it will take time for consumers to feel the tangible effects of this money, on a more psychological level, I speculate that a consumer that expects funds in the future is more prone to spend and prop up the economy with their dollar.

Consumer Weakness is Overstated

With the above mentioned, this is a perfect segue to discuss the current, as opposed to the future state of the consumer. After all, even after increased tax deductions, if the consumer is already struggling, it may be too late, or simply too little to support a failing economy.

However, I would like to reassure investors; I believe that this concern is incredibly overstated.

In my opinion, if investors want to find out the health of consumers, there is only one place they should go - which is to the experts that have focused on this industry for decades, and have been through multiple financial crises.

Of course, I am talking about the US's largest credit lenders, including JP Morgan Chase (JPM), American Express (AXP) and Capital One (COF).

In November of this year, I did a deep dive into this topic. The link to the in-depth article can be found here: Credit Card And BNPL Lenders Paint A Brighter-Than-Expected Picture Of The Economy

After analyzing some of the largest banks, and one BNPL player, being Klarna Plc. (KLAR), I can say that the outcome of the analysis completely went against my expectations. Hearing news of record-low consumer sentiment previously made me believe that the consumer is in fact facing some of the most dire times seen in the last few decades.

However, my research showed that this could not be farther from the truth.

While yes, it is true that the consumer is not prospering right now, as was seen in years immediately after COVID, where consumers were able to both save ample funds (due to the inability to spend their money anywhere), and also profit from Stimulus Checks, consumers are also far from struggling.

In particular, I would like to point out some metrics for Capital One, which is considered to serve the cohort of consumers with the lowest credit ratings, and as such the highest chance of default. In turn, this is often the first consumer to become delinquent in difficult times, as the bottom quartile of consumers tend to have the least in savings. Any declining credit performances should be clearly shown in the data within this major bank.

However, as can be seen above, the most fragile portion of the US consumer is actually improving in their credit metrics, with Net-Write Off Rates (the rates of banks considering their debt uncollectible) nearing to slightly below pre-pandemic levels, and Delinquency Rates (the percentage of balances or accounts overdue by 30+ days) trending just above this level.

To top it off, Capital One has actually released a major part of Reserves for Credit Losses, due to "recent favorable credit performance". When banks release reserves, it signals that they expect near term favorable conditions. The fact that the US's most vulnerable major credit lender is willing to do this at a time like this greatly reassures me as an investor.

Knowing the above two key pillars of my thesis around both the future and current state of the consumer, I am long on names that I believe perfectly encapsulate a current discount of "credit health", which I believe will re-rate as investors are shown increased earnings and significantly lower than expected delinquency and write off rates. For me, these names are Sezzle (SEZL) and Pagaya Technologies (PGY). Both of these names trade at a discount relative to their growth, and I am confident holding these names into 2026 and expect significant share price appreciation ahead.

"Follow the Money" Approach to the AI-Trade

I am far from the first to bring up the AI trade on Seeking Alpha. Despite that, I believe that following the AI trade is essential to any investor in 2026, being one of the largest shifts of this generation.

Although I believe we are in the first few innings of what would be considered the AI revolution in the future, some clear leaders have emerged, and many of these names have now become household names, whether it be the AI hardware providers, such as Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD (AMD), the hyperscalers like Google (GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META), or even the early AI software providers, such as Palantir (PLTR) and Snowflake (SNOW).

Despite my bullishness on the overall trend, I believe that the AI industry is significantly more complex than some investors may think. This industry, being the center of attention, is growing at unfathomable rates, and competition within the space is at an all-time high, with code-reds being sounded by some of the most prominent names within the space. Retail investors are deeply involved, making the markets increasingly volatile by trading the markets at record rates. And if that still wasn't enough, the Trump Administration has also begun to take part in this industry via the 10% stake in Intel (INTC).

Even if I had a team of analysts, I sincerely believe I would not be able to keep up with all the commotion of the industry. Simply put, I believe no one can predict the winners of the AI revolution. As such, my approach to the AI trade is different than others.

To me, my approach centers around "Following the Money", which to me means pursuing companies that benefit from any of the AI-related names, regardless of where they are located in the value chain. The best way to accomplish this is to follow the ever-increasing AI capex required to build out this industry.

As a reminder to investors, data centers can take over two years for the construction phase alone. This means the data centers currently finishing completion have most likely been funded by funds from the early 2020's. As a result, this means that the data center buildout announcements of today are targeting projects in the later part of this decade. This means that demand has already been secured for the next couple of years, at least.

As such, I believe many lucrative investment opportunities currently exist within the Industrials segment, although I believe that select Utilities and Energy players are bound to profit in a similar fashion.

More specifically to my portfolio, a lot of my investments within the AI trade are centered around small-to-mid-cap players, who have seen a massive acceleration in net income margins, often even out of unprofitable territory, as they have been finally given the chance to compete with the larger players due to simply put, unprecedented demand.

As an example, Power Solutions International (PSIX), is a mid-cap engine manufacturer and packager that has seen groundbreaking demand due to the AI data center buildout, as data center operators desperately look for power solutions to support their operations. Another small-cap player, Perma-Pipe International Holdings (PPIH), has seen a flood of data-center related contracts, with momentum only expected to continue due to the rise of liquid cooling in data centers.

I have written about both of these players on my page, with a successful "Strong Buy" track record on both names so far, and believe that the road to more profit is ahead.

Summary

All in all, my bullish thesis on the market revolves around three core points, which center around the consumer's future and current stance, in front of the backdrop of a massive surge of spending related to the buildout of AI.

I believe that the tax benefits introduced by President Trump's OBBBA will drive GDP in a favorable direction, and support any consumers that may currently be struggling via a process similar to that seen during the Covid Stimulus Checks.

Secondly, I strongly think that the consumer's current position is misstated. Major banks indicate that consumer health is somewhere closer to historical, pre-Covid norms. Additionally, banks such as Capital One, who deal with the most vulnerable cohort of the US consumer credit market, have actually released a major portion of reserves for credit losses, indicating that downside risk is not expected in the near term.

Lastly, the backdrop of AI remains a strong, with capital expenditures increasing to unforeseen levels. Despite this, I maintain a more cautious stance, and tend to stay away from fighting the competition and picking the winners. Instead, I have selectively chosen names that benefit from the physical buildout of AI, a trend for which the demand I believe is secured for years to come.

With that said, I would like to issue a "Buy" rating on the overall market, with a price target of $7,700 on the S&P 500, in-line with institutional estimates.

data.title: Taseko Mines: Well-Poised To Profit From The American Tilt
data.description:
Flourishing In A Different Orbit Altogether

The stock of Taseko Mines Limited (TGB), which has been developing a portfolio of copper-focused projects across the North American terrain has been one of the great success stories of the market in 2025. Granted, global metal and miners in general have experienced quite a bountiful year, generating returns of over 50%, but copper specialists in particular, have outperformed by 1.78x, with TGB taking the cake, generating astounding returns of 185%! Investors may also be interested to note that Taseko’s share price is now at levels last seen nearly 15 years ago!

What’s driving this fanfare, and is it sustainable as we head into 2026?

What’s Behind TGB’s Upward Thrust?

If passive observers of TGB were to take a cursory glance at some of the key financial metrics of this business for the first nine months of the current year, they may find it difficult to reconcile the jubilation around the TGB stock this year.

As you can see from the image below, revenue, EBITDA, earnings, cash flow, etc. haven’t been improving, but are all trending lower YoY, and not by an insignificant amount either. So, what gives?

Well, the excitement is primarily centred around the impending operations of TGB’s Florence Copper project (with annual runrate of 85mlbs p.a. at full capacity), with wellfield drilling having already restarted, leaching underway, and the first batch of copper cathodes expected to be processed in January 2026.

Working capital and CAPEX commitments related to this project shouldn’t be too overbearing now and even if there were to be some unforeseen spikes in expenditure, TGB is also well-armed with cash, having recently raised $170M of funds in October (the meteoric rise in TGB’s share price this year certainly helped them go down this route).

As noted in our previous coverage of TGB, this ISCR-oriented (in-situ copper recovery) project at Arizona is going to be very competitive from a cost angle (smaller work force compared to open pits, limited material handling, no need for smelting or refining of concentrates, lower energy costs and water use, etc), and will help raise the cash flow profile of TGB’s business. For context, TGB’s existing open pit operations at British Colombia (the 130mlbs Gibraltar mine) incurs cash costs of around$2.30/lb, whereas Florence’s leaching operations at Arizona, are poised to come in +50% lower at $1.10/lb!

The timing of Florence ramping up (next year’s production target is 35mlbps with a starting runrate of 1mlbps a month in early 2026 and finishing the year at a runrate of 6.5mlps) is also very opportune, as we are now in an environment where copper prices on the US COMEX are trading at a 2-3% premium (traditionally it’s been around 0-1.5%) over the London Metal Exchange [LME], with this differential expected to persist over the next 18 months.

Over 6 months from now, the US Commerce Department will advise Trump on whether he should impose a 15% tariff on refined copper imports from 2027. The probability of this happening is quite likely given that last month the Department of Interior placed copper, alongside 9 other metals in its list of critical minerals, highlighting the importance it wants to place on its domestic sources.

All in all, it’s hard not to see how these developments puts Florence in a rather advantageous spot, as it can cash in its production output next year at these attractive premiums, something which other global miners won’t be able to tap. You then couple that with its very low cash cost of operations, and it isn’t difficult to envisage how quickly TGB can ramp its profits and cash flow (operating profits are expected in Q2 itself, with free cash flow expected to flow shortly after).

If Florence can start generating free cash flow at a quicker than expected pace, we could also see TGB take steps to pare down its leverage, which is now at 3.7x (the spike in leverage since we last covered TGN, when it was at 2.2x, is more a function of a weak EBITDA effect, rather than debt going up). In FY27, TGB has stated their intention of bringing down their bond portfolio by around $75M.

While their Florence project is likely to be the centre of attraction in the near future, investors shouldn’t also forget TGB’s traditional open-pit operations at BC (the Gibraltar mine), with operating profits here very levered to a lift in copper prices. For context, even at average cash costs of $2.4/lb, a $0.5/lb spike in copper prices could potentially lift operating profits by CAD90M.

We feel copper prices in general are staring at a fairly robust outlook, given that the market deficit now is expected to snowball from 230k tons in 2025 to 407k tons in 2026.

Why Investors Shouldn’t Get Too Exuberant About The TGB Stock Now

We are not looking to play down Taseko Mines future prospects, which no doubt, look quite exciting, more so with Florence project expected to ramp next year, but investors shouldn’t also cast aside the valuations of the stock which makes one pause for thought, and ponder if a lot of the encouraging developments ahead, have already been digested.

To highlight, traditionally, this stock has been priced at single-digit forward EBITDA multiples of 7.3x (on average); now it is more than double that average at a whopping 16.2x! Sure, some of you may choose to dismiss the huge variance vs the stock’s historical average (which is still based on the rolling 1-year forward EBITDA estimates), but also take note of how it is now priced versus other key listed stocks from across the copper universe.

This is basically an industry where stocks are priced at average forward multiples of less than 12x. However, at 16.24x, TGB has even overtaken the largest (by market-cap) pure-play copper stock- Southern Copper Corp (SCCO), and is now trading at a near-40% premium over its peers.

Another reason to keep the exhilaration in check is because TGB now is one of the most overbought stocks within the broad metal and mining universe. For context, traditionally TGB’s relative strength ratio vs its mining peers has averaged around 0.039x, but at this juncture, it is almost 3x more than its mean, potentially making it vulnerable to some reversion.

Finally, also consider developments on TGB’s own monthly charts which suggest some caution would be useful now.

Such has been the exuberance around TGB, that December’s entire monthly candle is now trading above the stock’s upper bollinger band (a measure of overextension, as it is two standard deviations more than the 20-month rolling average). TGB is traditionally not used to trading entirely outside its upper BB on the monthly charts, and the last time it happened was back in May 2024 (highlighted in yellow), after which there was a retracement in the price action.

Investors may also want to consider that TGB is now hardly a breath away from hitting a price zone (highlighted in green) which previously (in 2007/08 and 2010/11) served as a stumbling block in facilitating further momentum. It’s also worth noting that the uptrend since August, has come on relatively lower volumes (compared to what was seen from to March to July) suggesting that conviction has been dipping as the stock moves high. Finally, also note that the RSI indicator has now hit overbought levels for the first time in decades.

All in all, we feel it wouldn’t necessarily be the best idea to get on the TGB bandwagon now; investors are advised to wait for a retracement in the price action, before getting on board.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Berkshire: One Beautiful Way To Play The Long XLI/XLU Thesis In 2026
data.description:
What This Article Is Really About

We have to close the circle.

We discussed a 2026 market outlook, which I anticipate to be a "revenge of the S&P 493". Basically, I believe 2026 will be a year where AI phase 2, cheaper energy, and a manufacturing rebound could lead to earnings growth breadth beyond the first AI beneficiaries, thus helping the rest of the market narrow the gap with the Mag 7. This is why I think that 2026 could be a year in favor of the equal-weighted index (RSP).

Then, we went one step further. It is clear that the market won't benefit as a whole. There will be winners and losers. Before identifying single stocks, I pointed out the two sectors I believe will do well next year and the two sectors I expect to lag behind. In short, I pointed out that Utilities (XLU) and Industrials (XLI) should outperform the market next year, while the likely laggards will be Energy (XLE) and Consumer Discretionary (XLY), with Real Estate also at risk (XLRE).

But now, we could ask ourselves whether there could be a real way to measure the durable returns we said we are expecting to appear. Moreover, since I believe that 2026 will be a year where math matters once again above narratives, if industrials and utilities are going to outperform, this won't happen because of multiple expansion, but because of cash flows and returns.

My idea here is simple: Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A, BRK.B) offers the opportunity to be long industrials and utilities, perhaps in an even more efficient way than sector ETFs. Thanks to its ownership of BNSF, BHE, and its manufacturing businesses, Berkshire Hathaway could be a great way to play the 2026 outlook I described while also keeping exposure to Big Tech (I love Alphabet) and to the financial sector, which I also expect to report decent, if not good, earnings.

So far, if you have happened to read one of my articles on Berkshire, you know that I have covered three main topics. First of all, I approached Berkshire to learn from Warren Buffett and how he conceived and carried out the purchase of Burlington Northern Santa Fe, the largest Class 1 railroad in the country (at least, until the UNP-NSC deal goes through). So, I took BNSF as a case study to outline the criteria and the metrics to use when deciding whether to invest in a Class 1 railroad or not. Since then, I have been covering all Class 1 railroads with this perspective. I then calculated the huge returns Berkshire had from its investment in BNSF.

A year ago, I expanded into the energy business and fully covered Berkshire Energy, highlighting once again how Buffett invested in utilities over time and how his understanding of the business developed.

The third area I covered was Berkshire's massive cash and T-Bills position, suggesting that, thanks to this cash, Berkshire would become a major repo lending player in case of a big recession, having the power to ultimately decide the fate of many businesses.

Now, this article gives me the opportunity to expand and introduce one more little tile of the huge mosaic Berkshire is: we will also deal with its holdings that fall under the category of manufacturing.

My readers know that when I cover Berkshire, my introductions are always a bit long because I have to explain how I am working my way into this company while developing a full understanding of this jewel that every investor should look at.

So, this article wants to suggest that another way to be long XLI and XLU could be by owning Berkshire's industrial throughput and regulated energy business. BNSF and Manufacturing, on one side, and BHE, on the other, could be two proxies for the sector ETFs we discussed a few days ago. In other words, this article suggests that Berkshire could be bought as a proxy that both helps us observe the real state of U.S. industrial activity and grid expansion and helps us focus on a selected number of businesses that have been carefully picked by Buffett and his lieutenants over several decades, offering us an ETF in disguise focused on cash flows and steady compounding. The advantage of investing through Berkshire would be that we immediately benefit from a lower beta and, usually, lower costs of financing.

A few numbers

If we look at the last Q3 report, we immediately see what is widely known: Berkshire's revenues come mainly from the insurance business. However, railroad, utilities, and energy account for $37.5B for the first nine months of the year. This means we are talking about roughly $50B in yearly revenue. It is larger than many S&P 500 companies. However, when we look at the operating income, we see that insurance reported $33.1B for the first nine months, translating into a 13.8% operating margin. The industrial and utility business reported an operating income of $7.3B, which represents a 19.5% margin. So, while the overall impact on Berkshire's revenues is 13.53%, the businesses we are going to discuss account for 18.3% of the company's operating profits, excluding investment gains. While not the largest source of profits, it surely is a meaningful part of Berkshire.

When we look at Berkshire's Manufacturing businesses, BNSF, and BHE, we have a three-layer view of the economy. Manufacturing shows up demand dynamics and the impact on margins; BNSF helps us feel the pulse of physical throughput in logistics; BHE shows us how capital can be invested at decent returns even in a regulated industry. Therefore, if truly Industrials and Utilities will outperform in 2026, we should see Berkshire Hathaway show some proof of our thesis.

Let's now look at each of the three businesses we highlighted, starting with BNSF.

BNSF: The Wide Moat

In most regions, Class 1 railroads create duopolies. BNSF competes with Union Pacific (UNP), and to ensure it keeps being competitive in case the UNP-NSC deal is approved, it has started to partner with CSX Corporation (CSX) to create a coast-to-coast streamlined service. Usually, because Class 1 railroads own networks that are nearly impossible to replicate, they are usually able to pass on inflation, both through the fuel surcharge program or by increasing their fees. Nonetheless, when volumes increase, their operational leverage is deployed at full speed.

Below, you see a screenshot from the latest Q3 report, where we see BNSF's results for Q3 and for the first nine months of the fiscal year. Now, with railroads, looking at revenues is always tricky because they include fuel surcharges. Therefore, we might see revenues go down, but only because fuel prices are decreasing. Nonetheless, even though fuel is in fact cheaper YoY, we see that revenues increased both for the quarter and for the first 9M. This means that BNSF is doing better than last year. Let's see why.

Buffett loves to concentrate on pre-tax earnings, which increased $66M YoY (+3.58%) and $337M for the first nine months (+6.76%). If we consider after-tax earnings, BNSF reported a quarterly increase of $66M (+4.8%) and of $376M (+10.0%) in the first nine months. The main drivers were pricing, operating efficiencies, and a lower tax rate.

When we look at the freight mix, we see that consumer products increased 2.2%. BNSF reported a lower revenue per car/unit, but slightly higher volumes, thanks to an increase in intermodal shipments (West Coast imports and automotive volumes increased). Therefore, operating revenues for the quarter were $2.1B, flat YoY.

Industrial products decreased 1.9% in Q3. This could immediately put us on guard, since we are talking about a manufacturing rebound. However, when we look at the 9M results, we see that volume is down 3.8%. Therefore, Q3 suggests that volume is increasing. However, industrial products managed to report revenues of $1.3B, which is flat YoY, meaning that pricing here matters. What drove volumes down was lower demand for construction products and for petroleum products. We have seen elsewhere that construction is mixed, with infrastructure spending up, but housing down.

Agricultural and energy products reported quarterly revenues of $1.6B, up 6.3% YoY, both because of slightly higher volumes and pricing. Grain exports ticked higher, helping the railroad's volumes.

Coal is particularly interesting. Quarterly revenues were unchanged at $795M, even though for the first 9M they reached $2.2B (+3.6% YoY). Volumes are down, but pricing is moving up as a consequence of higher natural gas prices.

When we consider the company's capital structure, we see that, so far this year, BNSF has repaid $900M of debt and issued $900M of long-term debt at 5.8% that will mature in 2056. Thus, we know there is no balance sheet stress, as the company has the liquidity it needs and can usually fund its capex internally.

So, we see that volumes were mixed across the four different categories, with strength in intermodal, automotive, and grains, while industrial products were soft. And yet, BNSF reported earnings growth mainly because of pricing gains. In this way, we see something that isn't often discussed. Even if we were to assume that we are in a late-cycle environment, a Class 1 railroad doesn't necessarily need volume growth to fuel its earnings growth. If the network is used well, pricing discipline and efficiency are more than enough to keep the company's earnings from deteriorating.

This is what we could call textbook cash-flow resilience of industrials, and it makes us think that XLI doesn't need the PMI to boom to report earnings growth. A scenario where industrial activity stabilizes is already favorable to industrials.

BHE: A Reinvesting Machine

Let's now move to energy. First of all, we have to make it clear that, even though Berkshire calls this its energy division, we are not talking about oil producers. We are talking about pipelines and utilities. It has interests in PacifiCorp, MidAmerican Energy Company, and NV Energy. It also has natural gas pipelines. Finally, BHE has a real estate brokerage business as one of its most interesting subsidiaries.

At first glance, when we look at BHE's Q3 2025 earnings, we may be disappointed. Its revenues declined by a tiny notch. But this is not volume collapsing. It was mainly due to higher costs that came from operations and maintenance, D&A, and interest expense. This means the asset base grew, and it is something I expect in a capital-intensive regulated utility. When we see D&A moving up, it just means that capital has been invested. Since interest rates are higher, a leak is in interest expense. However, YtD, we see BHE's pre-tax earnings up mainly because of lower input costs.

Usually, when capex flows into the asset base, it is a good starting sign for a utility because it means that it can invest, even at higher rates, and expect returns over time. Utilities must not be assessed on a quarter-by-quarter basis, since they can be highly volatile on this timeframe. But when they invest to enhance and grow their asset base, we can expect higher cash flow in the future.

BHE's Q3 after-tax earnings declined 8.6% YoY because of lower earnings from utilities, but YtD they still increased 9.6%. This is because of lower one-off costs linked to last year's wildfire risks. This forces utilities to book accruals upfront even if cash is not paid right away, and the final cost can be uncertain. These costs, however, have nothing to do with the operations of a utility and can be misleading if we don't carefully isolate them before looking at electricity demand, volumes, and, of course, pricing. This year, BHE reduced its accruals, and this made its earnings automatically go up. But we should not talk about earnings growth. It is more of a normalization.

So, it is better that we look at the electric utility margin, which was $2.6B in Q3 and $6.6B in the first nine months. They represent a $163M increase (+6.8%) and $593M increase (+9.9%) YoY, respectively. Bear in mind that the electric utility margin is an operating metric, not a bottom-line one. Therefore, it shows the key aspects of the business: rates, volumes, wholesale prices, and input costs.

The main drivers of this increase are therefore quite interesting: higher rates, higher volumes, and higher wholesale prices. As a matter of fact. BHE reported retail volumes up by 2.7% YoY, with MEC doing particularly well with a nice 9.8% increase. The weather was unfavorable, and this makes these results even more notable.

On the capital structure, BHE issued $3.1B of LT debt, at a rate of 6.4%, that should mature between 2035 and 2055. In the meantime, the company repaid $2.4B of debt.

Keep in mind that, unlike many utilities, BHE pays no dividend and retains all its earnings that are reinvested. Its capital structure, therefore, is optimized - in a true Buffett way - to grow the asset base. This is the ground to calculate the allowed regulated returns. Usually, ROEs for BHE range from 9% to 10.5%. Higher interest rates are already considered in the allowed return rates.

Basically, a regulated utility is allowed to earn a percentage per year on the equity portion of the assets it has invested in. When new investments are made, new assets are added to the base. The percentage remains the same, but the absolute number of the company's earnings grows. More capex means a larger return base. Regulators, however, also set a capital structure that they require. Typically, it is something like 50-50 equity/debt. This matters because a utility is allowed to earn a return on its equity that is summed with the possibility to pass on its interest debt expense. So the return rate is somewhat blended and becomes the true cost of capital that is embedded in the final rates customers pay.

Let's build a quick example that allows everyone to understand. Let's say that BHE invests $10B. The regulators have approved a 50-50 capital structure and allow an ROE of 10%. The cost of debt is around 6%. So, the allowed returns are calculated as follows:

Equity: $5B

Allowed ROE: 10%

Allowed earnings on equity: $500M

Debt: $5B

Interest rate: 6%

Interest expense: $300M

In this example, when BHE fixes its rates for its customers, it has to recover $800M, to which it can add a fee to pay for its operational costs and D&A. So, BHE earns a return on its equity, and it services its debt easily, without needing to rely on volume growth as much as other unregulated businesses would. Let's say that interest rates move up. Well, in this case, a utility files a rate case, and over time, it is usually allowed to adjust its customer rates. With interest rates, we should not say utilities are immune, but that they basically suffer from timing issues. Eventually, they recover their returns.

The intuition is then simple: utilities don't grow by selling more power; they grow by increasing their asset base.

To this extent, BHE is the perfect return generator because, unlike BNSF, it doesn't pay any dividend to its parent company. But this is exactly the strategy Buffet wants it to pursue, and BHE is free from investors demanding high yields, something that many utilities know very well.

Manufacturing

Let's now add the new piece: manufacturing.

This is a very complex area, with a huge variety of businesses. Berkshire splits it up into four main categories: industrial products, building products, consumer products, and service and retailing.

Industrial products

This group includes Precision Castparts Corporation (PCC), which makes metal components for end markets such as aerospace, power, and general industrial. We then have Lubrizol, specialized in chemicals, International Metalworking Companies (IMC), focused on metal cutting tools, Marmon, a conglomerate of over 100 manufacturing and service businesses, where we also find equipment leasing companies for the rail and mobile crane industries. Under industrial products, we also find exposure to agriculture through CTB International. Other big companies are LiquidPower Specialty Products, W&W|AFCO, and Bell Laboratories.

In Q3, revenues of this group were $9.5B and $28.1B for the first 9M, representing an increase of 5.8% for the quarter and 3.5% YtD. Again, we see that industrial activity seems to be accelerating. Pre-tax earnings also increased sharply, with Q3 reporting $362M (+24.3% YoY) and the 9M results being up by $550M YoY (+11.7%).

Building products

This group includes Clayton Homes (manufacturer of mobile homes). It also has Shaw, which focuses on flooring, Johns Manville, operating in insulation and roofing, Acme Brick (brick manufacturer), Benjamin Moore (paint), and MiTek (engineering products). This segment is seeing some softness, with quarterly sales down by $94M (-1.4% YoY) and by $220M YtD (-1.1%). However, pre-tax earnings improved by 7.6% in Q3 to $81M, while YtD they are still down by $76M (-2.4%). Berkshire admits that customer demand is soft and that pricing pressure is present. Nonetheless, the business is more or less holding up.

The building products group includes manufactured and site-built home construction and related lending and financial services (Clayton Homes). Other building products businesses include: flooring (Shaw), insulation, roofing and engineered products (Johns Manville), bricks and masonry products (Acme Brick), paint and coatings (Benjamin Moore), and residential and commercial construction and engineering products and systems (MiTek). Revenues of the building products group increased $94 million (1.4%) in the third quarter and $220 million (1.1%) in the first nine months of 2025 compared to 2024. Pre-tax earnings of the group increased $81 million (7.6%) in the third quarter and declined $76 million (2.4%) in the first nine months of 2025 compared to 2024. Certain of our building products businesses are experiencing slowing customer demand, as well as pricing pressures, attributable to prevailing general economic conditions and residential housing markets.

Consumer products is a group where Berkshire accounts for its leisure vehicles business, Forest River, its apparel and footwear holdings (Fruit of the Loom, Garan, H.H. Brown Shoe Group, and Brooks Sports), and the battery manufacturer Duracell. But we also have a toy company (Jazqares), and a jewelry company (Richline), as well as the custom picture framing products (Larson-Juhl), a famous acquisition that was closed in around 90 minutes.

Here, quarterly revenues declined the most, dropping by $237M (-6.2&), accelerating the pace versus the YtD performance, which is down 4%. Fruit of the Loom, Duracell, and Jazwares were the worst performers due to lower volumes. Brooks Sports, Forest River, and Richline, on the other hand, grew their sales.

The last group is service and retailing, of which I also want to show the revenue and pre-tax earnings summary.

Under "Service", Berkshire reports the results of NetJetx and FlightSafety, which are two aviation services businesses. But this is where Dairy Queen also falls, as well as the lease transportation equipment company XTRA and the furniture business CORT. Charter Brokerage, petroleum and chemical logistics services, and Business Wire (if we are on SA, we probably know this one) are part of the category as well.

Then we find McLane, which is a wholesale distribution business that delivers groceries and non-food products to retailers and restaurants. It also distributes spirits.

Retailing includes Berkshire Hathaway Automotive, a network of 80 auto dealerships. Here, the real business is to sell insurance contracts. There are also four home furnishing companies (NFM, R.C. Willey, Star Furniture, and Jordan’s). Then, we find three jewelry businesses (Borsheims, Helzberg, and Ben Bridge), Pampered Chef (higher-end kitchen tools), Oriental Trading Company (supplies for parties and schools, as well as toys) and Detlev Louis Motorrad (“Louis”), a German retailer for motorcycle accessories.

But the most popular holding here is See's Candies, of which Buffett is truly fond.

Pilot is a company that is in the travel centers business, but it also works in the large wholesale fuel business. For accounting reasons, it is usually treated separately from the rest.

As we can see, Berkshire's holdings are extremely different from each other. When we group them together, we see that "Service and retailing" grew YoY, both on a quarterly and a YtD basis. All four groups performed well, even though Pilot is down YtD. This is also the least profitable business so far, while Service and Retailing have decent pre-tax margins.

Closing The Circle

Across Manufacturing, BHE, and BNSF, Berkshire is not reporting booming activity. But this is exactly where I find Berkshire as an interesting play to go long Industrials and Utilities as we enter 2026. We see more important signs that are relevant for my thesis: improving economics and stabilization.

Manufacturing shows that demand, although it may be uneven, has not collapsed. In particular, industrial products are doing well and reporting margin expansion. The Building Products Group confirms that the housing market remains somewhat of a drag. BNSF strengthens the message from a different perspective, while volumes are still mixed, pricing discipline is making the railroad grow its earnings. If industrial activity simply stabilizes next year, Industrials won't need very heroic assumptions to be more profitable when comparing them YoY.

The framework is completed by BHE. We saw that utility margins are expanding and that capital continues to be invested to grow the asset base. In this environment, BHE is not a bond proxy, as many utilities are often considered, but it is the best platform to capture regulated returns.

Manufacturing tells us that demand and margins should improve. BNSF tells us that transportation activity is holding up. BHE offers us decent returns even in a higher-rate environment.

If next year is going to be the year when earnings breadth becomes more important than narratives, then I keep thinking that Industrials and Utilities won't need to report staggering results to outperform. They only need to keep doing what they have been doing, as Berkshire's results suggest.

So, a way to play the long XLI/XLU thesis could be to simply invest in Berkshire. In this way, we own industrial throughput while benefiting also from a low beta and a disciplined company focused on compounding.

We have one last brick to add, which actually strengthens the long BRK.B thesis. Berkshire has significant stakes in Apple and, now, in Alphabet. I don't think Berkshire is betting on who wins AI, even though I think it is well aware of Alphabet's vertical integration and the advantages from its 3B+ user base. But by adding Apple and Alphabet to the picture, we know that by buying Berkshire, we own a variety of companies that can benefit not only from AI but also from the industrial consequences of AI (think of the iPhone 17 upgrade cycle). AI could be a demand accelerant that will benefit a lot of Berkshire's holdings.

Secondly, Berkshire's massive T-Bills position offers downside protection, earnings support, and optionality at the same time. This means that the company has a meaningful liquidity buffer and that it makes extra earnings thanks to the higher rates that T-Bills pay. In case of a crisis, Berkshire can deploy its cash at will. So, it would be a big mistake to consider this cash dead money.

When we look at Berkshire's valuation, I see a stock trading at a fwd P/E of 23 and a fwd P/Book ratio of 1.5. I find these very reasonable multiples to buy safely all the assets we discussed, especially when the 2026 outlook favors most of the companies included there. To me, Berkshire represents a nice long-term buy that is now offering a very interesting entry point as the setup looks favorable for many of its holdings.
